title: Finite-time transitions in optimal control and non-equilibrium relaxation
authors: Jan Meibohm, Samuel Monter, Sarah A. M. Loos, Clemens Bechinger
abstract: We theoretically and experimentally study finite-time optimal control of a colloidal particle steered through a spatially inhomogeneous environment, modeled by a position-dependent energetic cost at the final state. The competition between this state-dependent penalty and path-dependent dissipation gives rise to a sharp transition in the control strategy at a critical control duration. We further show that this transition can be linked to a dynamical phase transition in nonequilibrium relaxation after a quench, where the control cost maps onto the rate function governing rare trajectories.
summary: This paper explores how the duration of a control process determines the optimal strategy for moving a particle through a complex environment. It discovers that at a specific critical time, the optimal strategy abruptly changes from doing nothing to actively steering the particle. The researchers prove that this change in control is mathematically equivalent to a dynamical phase transition seen in non-equilibrium relaxation. The findings were verified using experimental optical tweezers on colloidal particles.
main_problem: Investigating finite-time transitions in the optimal control of particles in structured environments and establishing a link between these control transitions and dynamical phase transitions in non-equilibrium relaxation.
main_result: The study identifies a sharp transition in optimal control strategy at a critical duration (tc) where the system switches from a symmetric, zero-control solution to a symmetry-broken, active steering solution. This transition is shown to be the dual of a finite-time dynamical phase transition (FTDPT) in relaxation processes.
method: The authors combine theoretical frameworks, including the Pontryagin principle and Large Deviation Theory, with experimental measurements using optical tweezers, acousto-optical deflectors, and digital video microscopy.
model_or_system: A colloidal silica particle in a water-glycerol mixture manipulated via optical tweezers within a symmetric double-well potential. The dynamics are modeled using an overdamped Langevin equation.
key_observables: Optimal control protocol (lambda*(t)), optimal trajectory (u*(t)), stochastic work (W), order parameter (|u*_f|), and susceptibility (chi_t).
important_parameters: Control duration (tf), critical time (tc), particle relaxation time (tau_p), trap stiffness (kappa), and the potential barrier height (V0).
main_assumptions: The analysis assumes the weak-noise limit (kB T << V0) and uses the Euler-Machlup functional to identify optimal fluctuations.
figures_summary: Figures depict the jumps in the optimal control protocol at boundary points and the scaling behavior of the susceptibility near the critical point.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of finite-time control, describes the experimental setup and Langevin model, presents the theoretical derivation of the control transition using the Pontryagin principle, demonstrates the mapping to dynamical phase transitions in relaxation, and concludes with the experimental verification of these transitions.
why_interesting: This work is highly relevant for researchers in open quantum systems and many-body dynamics as it provides an experimental platform to study rare event physics and dynamical phase transitions without the sampling bottlenecks typically associated with observing rare fluctuations.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. A particle (blue) is driven by a harmonic trap (red) at constant mean horizontal velocity v∥toward an obstacle (green), characterized by an energetic cost V(x). The trap controls the lateral velocity v⊥to minimize the total work performed on the particle.

FIG. 2. Optimal control protocols for different average initial posi- tions u0 (dashed) and experimental trajectories [solid red in (a), blue in (b)], with process durations: (a) tf = 0.47tc, and (b) t f = 1.65tc. (c) W ∗ t f at u0 = 0 as a function of tf from experiments (markers) and theory (line) for V0 = κx2m = 151kBT (d) W ∗ t f as a function of u0 at tf = 0.47tc (red) and t f = 1.65tc (blue). Results from experi- ments (markers) and theory (lines) for V0 = κx2m = 124kBT. ˜V(uf ) is shown in green. Inset: |u∗ f | as a function of the control time t f (yellow) from experiments (markers) and theory (solid line).

FIG. 3. Results of the free-relaxation experiment, post-processed with the reweighing method, for V0 = 40kBT from 2000 trajectories. (a) Rate function V ∗ t f as a function of x f , for different t f . (b) Or- der parameter density P(|x0|,tf ) as a function of t from experiments (heat map) and |x∗ 0| from theory (solid line). P(|x0|,tf ) is scaled to unity at each tf to improve visibility. (c) Trajectory density of re- laxation trajectories that reach x f = 0 at different times t f from ex- periment (heat map), combined for tf = 0.55tc and t = 2tc. Solid lines show the theoretical predictions. (d) Susceptibility of the order parameter x0 as a function tf at V0 = 10kBT, 20kBT, 30kBT, and...
